description: Editorial raw-content overrides applied during current-runtime generation. rawOfficialTextSource points back to raw-content-bank entries so source text is not duplicated here.

home-and-wandering::navigation at sea me.title: Finding the Sea: Tools That Made the Ocean Measurable

quizQuestions.prompt: What did the magnetic compass make easier for sailors?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Finding direction at sea
wrongAnswers: Predicting every storm perfectly
Repairing damaged ships
Avoiding all danger forever

quizQuestions.prompt: What was one major benefit of latitude and longitude?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: They helped sailors describe position more precisely on Earth
wrongAnswers: They replaced the need for ships
They made oceans smaller
They worked only near Europe

quizQuestions.prompt: Why were tools like the astrolabe, sextant, and chronometer so important for long voyages?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: They helped sailors estimate position and navigate more accurately over long distances
wrongAnswers: They replaced maps completely
They made ships move faster than wind
They were mainly decorative objects for captains

quizQuestions.prompt: How did navigation tools help change the relationship between humans and the ocean?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: They turned open water from something frightening and uncertain into something measurable and trackable
wrongAnswers: They made the ocean completely safe
They removed the need for skilled sailors
They proved that all voyages were successful

quizQuestions.prompt: Why do navigation tools matter historically beyond sailing itself?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Because they helped turn unknown space into something humans could control, which also supported exploration, trade, and empire
wrongAnswers: Because they mattered only to scientists
Because they ended all conflict at sea
Because they were invented only for maps

quizQuestions.prompt: What is magnetoreception?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: The ability to sense Earth's magnetic field
wrongAnswers: The ability to see only at night
A way of measuring wind speed
A machine humans put inside animals

quizQuestions.prompt: What does olfactory navigation use?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Smell
wrongAnswers: Only sight
Heat from the ground
Human-made maps

quizQuestions.prompt: Why do scientists study both instinct and navigation in animals?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Because animal movement may be automatic in some ways and still involve complex orientation skills
wrongAnswers: Because only humans can really navigate
Because migration is mostly random
Because instinct and navigation can never overlap

quizQuestions.prompt: Why might human activity make migration harder for animals?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: It can disrupt the natural signals they use, such as light, sound, smell, landmarks, or magnetic cues
wrongAnswers: It teaches animals to stop moving forever
It makes every route shorter
It gives animals too many options

quizQuestions.prompt: What is the most important idea behind animal navigation?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Many animals do far more than simply wander, using complex systems to move through the world and sometimes return with astonishing accuracy
wrongAnswers: Animal migration is mostly accidental
Navigation becomes real only when humans use technology
Animals follow paths only because people trained them

quizQuestions.prompt: What is one main idea behind traditional wayfinding?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: People can navigate by reading the world around them, not only by using instruments
wrongAnswers: Maps are useless in every case
Only sailors can learn directions
Traditional navigation is mostly imaginary

quizQuestions.prompt: What did Polynesian navigators use to help find their way?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Stars, ocean swells, winds, and observations of the natural world
wrongAnswers: Satellites and radar
Highways and road signs
Only written maps

quizQuestions.prompt: What makes Polynesian wayfinding especially impressive?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: It used careful observation and memory instead of modern instruments
wrongAnswers: It worked only in calm weather
It depended on airplane routes
It was mainly a ceremonial performance

quizQuestions.prompt: How is traditional navigation different from relying on GPS?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: It depends more on memory, attention, and reading the environment directly
wrongAnswers: It removes all uncertainty
It works only in the past
It is always faster than digital tools

quizQuestions.prompt: What is the strongest idea behind non-instrument navigation?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: A map does not always need to exist on paper or a screen; it can live in memory, skill, and close attention to the world
wrongAnswers: Phones make all older knowledge worthless
Traditional knowledge matters only as legend
Without GPS, people cannot really know where they are

home-and-wandering::what if the world st.title: Should Some Parts of the World Stay Foggy?

quizQuestions.prompt: What does Fog of World try to recreate?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: The feeling of discovering places gradually instead of seeing everything at once
wrongAnswers: A weather forecast system
A driving test simulator
A social media map of restaurants

quizQuestions.prompt: What is one possible benefit of not knowing everything before a journey?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Surprise and discovery may feel more real
wrongAnswers: It guarantees safety
It removes all need for planning
It makes every trip shorter

quizQuestions.prompt: Why might some people enjoy keeping parts of the world 'foggy'?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Because uncertainty and discovery can make travel feel more meaningful
wrongAnswers: Because getting lost is always safe
Because maps are bad for the brain
Because people should never prepare

quizQuestions.prompt: What bigger question does an app like Fog of World raise?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Whether too much easy access to information can reduce the feeling of exploration
wrongAnswers: Whether maps should be banned
Whether travel is better without destinations
Whether games are more important than geography

quizQuestions.prompt: What is the strongest idea behind the appeal of keeping some places 'foggy'?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Sometimes having less immediate knowledge can make exploration feel deeper, more active, and more memorable
wrongAnswers: All information should be hidden from travelers
Technology always ruins experience
The best journeys are always unplanned

quizQuestions.prompt: Why is the airport in The Terminal such a powerful setting?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Because it is a place built for movement that becomes a place of suspension
wrongAnswers: Because airports are always homes
Because airports have no laws
Because the movie is mostly about tourism

quizQuestions.prompt: What made Mehran Karimi Nasser's case so unusual?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: He became stuck in an airport transit zone with no clear way to enter or return home
wrongAnswers: He chose to live at the airport for fun
He was the first person ever to miss a flight
He owned the airport where he stayed

quizQuestions.prompt: What makes someone in airport limbo especially vulnerable?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: They may be stuck between legal systems, with unclear status and nowhere they are fully allowed to go
wrongAnswers: They automatically become citizens of the airport
They are safe because airports solve political problems
They can simply choose any country

quizQuestions.prompt: Why can an airport become a liminal space in a political sense?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Because it can trap someone between departure and arrival, legality and illegality, entry and refusal
wrongAnswers: Because airports exist outside geography
Because planes erase national borders
Because customs officers are optional

quizQuestions.prompt: What is the strongest overall idea shown by cases like The Terminal, Nasseri, and Snowden?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: A transit space can become a place where identity, belonging, and movement all break down at once
wrongAnswers: Airports are the best place to live long-term
Governments should always follow movie plots
The main problem with airports is boredom

home-and-wandering::reaching the border.title: Reaching the Border Is Not the Same as Being Accepted

quizQuestions.prompt: What does the case of the St. Louis show?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Reaching a destination does not always mean being allowed to enter
wrongAnswers: Ships always find safe harbor
Refugees were welcomed everywhere in 1939
Borders are only symbolic

quizQuestions.prompt: Why is the St. Louis remembered as such a tragic case?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Because many of the refugees who were denied entry later died in the Holocaust
wrongAnswers: Because the ship got lost at sea forever
Because the passengers refused rescue
Because no country knew the ship existed

quizQuestions.prompt: What makes border policy morally difficult in refugee situations?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Governments weigh security, law, resources, and human need at the same time
wrongAnswers: Borders have no effect on human lives
Refugee decisions are always simple
Every country has the same obligations and the same capacity

quizQuestions.prompt: What is one major difference between a map border and a real border?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: A map border is just a line, but a real border involves political choices with human consequences
wrongAnswers: A real border exists only at airports
A map border is always more powerful
There is no real difference

quizQuestions.prompt: What is the strongest idea behind refugee refusal cases like the St. Louis?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: A border is not just a place of arrival; it is a moral and political test of who is allowed through and why
wrongAnswers: Countries should never make migration policy
Destinations matter more than human lives
A closed border is always the fairest border

home-and-wandering::poems of the immigra.title: Poems of Migration and In-Between Lives

quizQuestions.prompt: What do these migration poems mainly explore?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: The emotional experience of migration, belonging, and living between places
wrongAnswers: How to fill out immigration forms
The history of ships only
Tourism marketing

quizQuestions.prompt: What is one reason poetry works well for migration?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: It can express feelings like hope, fear, memory, and loss in a concentrated way
wrongAnswers: It avoids all emotion
It gives exact policy answers
It makes migration simpler than it is

quizQuestions.prompt: Why does poetry work especially well for the immigrant experience?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Because it can hold uncertainty, identity, longing, and contradiction all at once
wrongAnswers: Because poems are always easier than history
Because poetry avoids politics completely
Because migration is mainly a literary invention

quizQuestions.prompt: What does a poem like 'The New Colossus' show about migration?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: That a poem can shape the symbolic meaning of a nation's welcome to newcomers
wrongAnswers: That poems matter only in classrooms
That migration is only an economic issue
That all immigrants are seen the same way

quizQuestions.prompt: What is the strongest overall idea behind migration poetry?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: The immigrant experience can be tied to one place and still speak to broader human feelings of exile, hope, and becoming
wrongAnswers: Migration poems matter only in the United States
Poetry gives exact border solutions
All immigrants experience movement in exactly the same way

home-and-wandering::artists of the in be.title: Art, Music, and the Migrant In-Between

quizQuestions.prompt: What do these works mainly share?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: They explore what it feels like to live between homes, places, or identities
wrongAnswers: They are all travel advertisements
They are all about tourism success
They all avoid the idea of migration

quizQuestions.prompt: Why can music and visual art be powerful for representing migration?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: They can show feelings of distance, displacement, longing, and identity in ways that facts alone cannot
wrongAnswers: They are more objective than history
They remove politics from the topic
They matter only if they are realistic

quizQuestions.prompt: Why is Jacob Lawrence's Migration series especially relevant to this topic?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Because it turns movement and displacement into a visual story about collective experience
wrongAnswers: Because it is a road map
Because it explains customs law
Because it shows tourists on holiday

quizQuestions.prompt: What makes a song like 'Somos Mas Americanos' or 'Immigrant Song' different from a policy debate on migration?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: It expresses identity, emotion, tension, or protest through voice, rhythm, and performance rather than through law or statistics
wrongAnswers: It gives more exact legal definitions
It avoids all meaning beyond entertainment
It proves migration is only a musical topic

quizQuestions.prompt: What is the strongest overall idea behind artistic works about migration?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Art can make the migrant's in-between condition visible in ways that facts, borders, and policies alone cannot
wrongAnswers: Music and painting are less useful than history
Migration is mainly a legal issue, not a human one
The only migrant story worth telling is a successful one

quizQuestions.prompt: What is the main difference between adolescence and the idea of a teenager?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Adolescence is a developmental stage, while 'teenager' is a more recent social category
wrongAnswers: Adolescence exists only in humans, while teenagers exist in many animals
Teenagers are biological, but adolescence was invented by schools
There is no real difference between the two

quizQuestions.prompt: What does the prefrontal cortex help with?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Planning, self-control, and thinking ahead
wrongAnswers: Digestion and breathing only
Remembering every dream perfectly
Choosing eye color

quizQuestions.prompt: Why is the modern idea of the teenager considered relatively new?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Because modern schooling, youth culture, spending money, and greater independence made young people more visible as a separate group
wrongAnswers: Because no one under 20 existed before the 1900s
Because hormones were discovered only recently
Because earlier societies forgot to count ages properly

quizQuestions.prompt: Why is the sentence 'the brain fully develops at 25' too simple?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Because brain development is gradual and does not suddenly stop at one exact age
wrongAnswers: Because the brain is fully finished by 13
Because only memory changes after childhood
Because the frontal lobes are not part of the brain

quizQuestions.prompt: What is the strongest idea behind the history of teenagers?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Humans have always gone through adolescence, but modern societies turned that stage into a distinct social identity called the teenager
wrongAnswers: Teenagers are a purely biological invention of the 20th century
Adolescence only became possible once schools were built
Teen culture matters only because of pop music

quizQuestions.prompt: What idea is built into the word 'adolescent'?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Growing toward adulthood
wrongAnswers: Being exactly the same as an adult
Refusing all responsibility forever
Turning into a number

quizQuestions.prompt: Why is the word 'teenager' especially tied to English?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Because it depends on the English number pattern from thir-teen to nine-teen
wrongAnswers: Because only English-speaking countries have young people
Because Latin created the word 'teenager'
Because English invented adolescence itself

quizQuestions.prompt: What is one important difference between the words 'adolescent' and 'teenager'?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: 'Adolescent' sounds more developmental, while 'teenager' sounds more social and cultural
wrongAnswers: One is true and the other is false
One describes children and the other describes old people
They always mean exactly the same thing in every language

quizQuestions.prompt: What does comparing these words across languages help show?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: That life stages are shaped not only by biology, but also by language and culture
wrongAnswers: That all languages divide the teenage years in exactly the same way
That translation makes adolescence disappear
That age categories are purely random

quizQuestions.prompt: What is the main purpose of a rite of passage?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: To mark an important change in status or stage of life
wrongAnswers: To make birthdays last longer
To replace all school exams
To prove that adults enjoy formal clothing

quizQuestions.prompt: What is one key difference between a cultural rite and a secular rite?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: A cultural rite usually carries tradition or shared identity, while a secular rite is often tied to modern institutions or everyday life
wrongAnswers: Cultural rites are serious, but secular rites are fake
Secular rites only exist in one country
Cultural rites always happen before age 10

quizQuestions.prompt: Why can a driver's license or a first paycheck feel like a rite of passage even without a formal ceremony?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Because they signal new independence, rights, or responsibilities
wrongAnswers: Because they are older than religion
Because they automatically make someone wise
Because paperwork is the purest form of adulthood

quizQuestions.prompt: Why do many societies create special ceremonies for growing up?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Because they turn a slow and messy change into a clear public moment people can recognize
wrongAnswers: Because adulthood happens instantly everywhere
Because teenagers cannot understand change without costumes
Because every culture uses the same ritual system

quizQuestions.prompt: What is the strongest idea behind rites of passage?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: They help communities give a visible shape to a transition that is often gradual and uncertain in real life
wrongAnswers: They create adulthood instantly and completely
They prove that all cultures define adulthood in exactly the same way
They matter only if expensive gifts are involved

quizQuestions.prompt: What do many works about adolescence have in common?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: They show growing up as confusing, uneven, and emotionally complicated
wrongAnswers: They all celebrate adulthood as easy and complete
They are all about school grades only
They all prove that growing up is a bad idea

quizQuestions.prompt: Why can poetry, stories, and songs be especially powerful for showing adolescence?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Because they can express mixed feelings and identity struggles more vividly than a simple definition can
wrongAnswers: Because art is always more factual than science
Because music removes emotion from a topic
Because adulthood can only be explained by artists

quizQuestions.prompt: What does a phrase like 'not a girl, not yet a woman' suggest about adolescence?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: It is an in-between state where someone is no longer a child but not fully settled into adulthood
wrongAnswers: It is a stage where nothing changes
It means adulthood starts the moment someone turns 13
It proves identity is always clear during youth

quizQuestions.prompt: Why do so many artistic works focus on the middle years instead of a clean 'before' and 'after'?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Because growing up often happens through uncertainty, conflict, and partial change rather than one neat transformation
wrongAnswers: Because artists dislike endings
Because adolescence has no emotional intensity
Because adulthood is too simple to write about

quizQuestions.prompt: What is the strongest idea behind artistic works about growing up?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Adulthood often feels less like reaching a destination and more like trying on roles, making mistakes, and slowly changing
wrongAnswers: Becoming an adult happens in one exact moment for everyone
Teenagers grow only when adults force them to
The best way to grow up is to avoid all confusion

quizQuestions.prompt: What does the word 'adulting' usually mean?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Doing everyday grown-up tasks in a way that often feels effortful or awkward
wrongAnswers: Becoming old overnight
Refusing all responsibility
Turning school into a full-time job

quizQuestions.prompt: Which of these is a typical example of 'adulting'?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Scheduling a dentist appointment or paying bills
wrongAnswers: Playing hide-and-seek
Forgetting all your homework forever
Sleeping through every obligation

quizQuestions.prompt: Why do people often use the word 'adulting' jokingly?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Because it lets them talk about adult responsibilities while showing they still feel a bit unsure or distant from 'real adulthood'
wrongAnswers: Because adult life is always easy and funny
Because only children are allowed to make jokes
Because rent and paperwork are naturally hilarious

quizQuestions.prompt: What does the popularity of the word 'adulting' suggest about adulthood today?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Many people experience adulthood less as a fixed identity and more as a set of tasks they are learning to manage
wrongAnswers: No one wants responsibility anymore
Adulthood has disappeared completely
Only millennials ever felt uncertain

quizQuestions.prompt: What is the strongest idea behind the word 'adulting'?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Adulthood may feel less like a sudden identity and more like a collection of habits, duties, and repeated performances
wrongAnswers: A person becomes an adult only when they say they are
Adulting means the same thing in every culture and generation
Being an adult is mostly about buying appliances

quizQuestions.prompt: What do the works in this part mainly have in common?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: They explore ways of thinking about death as a final threshold or transition
wrongAnswers: They all say death should be ignored
They are all written from the exact same religious point of view
They all describe airports and ships literally

quizQuestions.prompt: Which poem turns death into a calm carriage ride?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Emily Dickinson's 'Because I could not stop for Death'
wrongAnswers: 'Ulysses'
'In the End'
'We Real Cool'

quizQuestions.prompt: What does it mean to describe death as a threshold?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: It suggests death can be seen as a crossing or transition, not only as a stopping point
wrongAnswers: It means death is not serious
It proves every writer agrees about the afterlife
It turns death into a scientific experiment

quizQuestions.prompt: Why do artists return so often to the subject of death?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Because art helps people imagine, question, and emotionally face what is hardest to explain directly
wrongAnswers: Because death is easier to understand than everyday life
Because all art must be tragic
Because audiences dislike hopeful themes

quizQuestions.prompt: What is the strongest overall idea behind works about the approach of death?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Human beings use art to imagine death not only as an ending, but also as a passage, transformation, or final form of meaning
wrongAnswers: All great art agrees completely about what death is
Death matters only when it is dramatic or violent
The best response to death is always silence

entries.title: When Power Is Supposed to Be Temporary

links.label: Caretaker government - Australian Parliamentary Education Office

entries.studentExplanation: A caretaker government runs a country during an election period or another temporary transition, but it is supposed to keep the system stable rather than launch major new changes. This entry helps students understand what these governments usually do, who staffs them, and why they are meant to stay limited, neutral, and temporary.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this explains a political system designed for the in-between period before a new government takes full control. At the section level, it introduces The End Is Nearish by showing that even before the end of a government, power can already become transitional. At the year-theme level, it connects strongly to thresholds, endings, and transition because caretaker governments exist between one stable administration and the next.
entries.takeaway: A political system may need an officially limited form of power to guide the country safely through an in-between moment.
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether short-term neutral governments can reduce mistrust during elections and fragile transfers of power.
entries.counterargument: Someone could argue that caretaker governments may still become political actors themselves and are not automatically neutral just because they are temporary.

items.title: Keeping the state stable during a transition
items.note: A caretaker government manages the country during an election period or another temporary transfer of power. Its job is usually to keep the system running smoothly while avoiding major new decisions that should belong to the next fully empowered administration.

quizQuestions.prompt: What is the main job of a caretaker government?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: To keep government running during a temporary transition without making major new decisions
wrongAnswers: To rewrite the constitution before the election
To replace parliament permanently
To cancel elections

quizQuestions.prompt: Why are caretaker governments usually expected to avoid big new policies?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Because they are meant to preserve stability until a fully empowered government takes office
wrongAnswers: Because they are always unelected monarchies
Because they have no ministers at all
Because they exist only during war

quizQuestions.prompt: What makes a caretaker government different from an ordinary long-term government?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: It is temporary and is expected to act with restraint during a political transition
wrongAnswers: It has unlimited power for a short time
It rules without laws
It exists only in presidential systems

quizQuestions.prompt: Why might some countries choose to use caretaker governments more often?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Because temporary neutral management can reduce mistrust during elections or fragile transfers of power
wrongAnswers: Because voters dislike all permanent governments
Because short governments are always more efficient
Because elections work best without rules

quizQuestions.prompt: What is the strongest idea behind caretaker governments?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: A political system may need an officially limited form of power to guide the country safely through an in-between moment
wrongAnswers: The best government is one that never changes
Temporary governments should always stay forever
Transitions matter less than stability

entries.title: Lame Ducks and Fading Power
entries.studentExplanation: A lame duck leader is still officially in charge but is already losing influence because everyone knows the end is coming. This often happens when a term limit is near, when a successor is expected, or when allies start planning for the future without that leader. This entry asks whether term limits weaken leadership too early - or whether the real danger is letting leaders stay forever.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this helps students understand how power can shrink before it formally ends. At the section level, it expands the idea of political endings from caretaker systems to leaders whose authority fades in public view. At the year-theme level, it connects to endings and transition because it shows that decline often begins before the official finish line.
entries.takeaway: The end of formal power and the end of real influence are often not the same thing.
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether term limits protect democracy enough to justify the weaker authority that often appears near the end of a leader's last term.
entries.counterargument: Someone could argue that a weaker ending phase is still better than allowing leaders to accumulate too much long-term power.

items.title: Why power can weaken before office officially ends
items.note: A lame duck leader is still in office, but public attention has already started moving toward the future. When succession becomes visible, allies, rivals, and institutions may begin adjusting long before the formal ending arrives.

quizQuestions.prompt: What is a lame duck leader?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: A leader who is still in office but is losing power because the end of their term is near
wrongAnswers: A leader who cannot pass any laws at all
A leader chosen by the military
A leader who governs only during emergencies

quizQuestions.prompt: Why do lame duck periods often happen near the end of a final term?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Because attention shifts toward the next possible leader
wrongAnswers: Because the constitution stops working
Because voters are not allowed to care anymore
Because leaders lose their title immediately

quizQuestions.prompt: What is one argument in favor of term limits even if they create lame ducks?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: They can reduce the risk of one person holding power for too long
wrongAnswers: They guarantee perfect successors
They make politics less competitive
They remove all corruption

quizQuestions.prompt: What is one argument against removing term limits just to avoid lame ducks?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: A stronger ending phase may not be worth the long-term risk of power becoming too permanent
wrongAnswers: Every long-serving leader becomes better automatically
Democracy works best when one person stays forever
Succession plans are unnecessary

quizQuestions.prompt: What is the strongest idea behind the lame duck problem?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: The end of formal power and the end of real influence are often not the same thing
wrongAnswers: Term limits always destroy democracy
Leaders should never know when they will leave
Political systems cannot survive transitions

entries.title: If No One Knew Who Was Next
entries.studentExplanation: Elizabeth I delayed naming a successor because she feared that once people focused on the next ruler, loyalty to the current one would weaken. This entry asks a smart political question: does naming the next leader too early stabilize a system - or start the transfer of power before the old leader is gone? It is really about when a transition begins: at departure, or much earlier in people's minds.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this explores the political risk of succession becoming visible too soon. At the section level, it continues the section's interest in what happens when endings are expected before they happen. At the year-theme level, it connects to thresholds and transition because succession creates an in-between moment where the future leader may already shape the present.
entries.takeaway: Knowing who comes next can both stabilize a system and quietly weaken the person still in charge.
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether clear succession plans strengthen stability or start moving loyalty away from the current leader too early.
entries.counterargument: Someone could argue that keeping succession unclear may preserve authority in the short term but increase instability once the real transition finally arrives.

items.title: Why delaying a successor can preserve authority
items.note: Elizabeth I's logic was simple: once the next ruler becomes visible, the present ruler may begin to fade in importance. This card helps students think about whether succession begins officially - or psychologically.

quizQuestions.prompt: Why might a leader delay naming a successor?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Because public attention might shift away from the current leader too early
wrongAnswers: Because succession never matters in politics
Because people dislike continuity
Because it makes laws disappear

quizQuestions.prompt: What is one possible advantage of keeping the next leader unknown for longer?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: It may preserve the authority of the current leader while they remain in office
wrongAnswers: It guarantees a better successor
It ends all power struggles
It removes the need for succession rules

quizQuestions.prompt: What is one possible downside of hiding succession too long?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: It can create uncertainty and competition when the transition finally arrives
wrongAnswers: It makes politics too calm
It ensures the public feels safer
It prevents all rivalries

quizQuestions.prompt: What political idea sits underneath this problem?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Power may begin transferring in people's minds before it transfers officially
wrongAnswers: A leader's authority never depends on public attention
Succession matters only in monarchies
Future leaders should always campaign secretly

quizQuestions.prompt: What is the strongest idea behind delayed succession?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Knowing who comes next can both stabilize a system and quietly weaken the person still in charge
wrongAnswers: The best system is one with no succession at all
Mystery always improves leadership
Transitions should never be discussed

entries.title: The Clock That Measures Doom

links.label: Why the Doomsday Clock matters - Business Insider

entries.studentExplanation: The Doomsday Clock is not a scientific machine that predicts the exact end of the world. It is a symbolic warning device created to show how close humanity may be to catastrophic danger, especially through war, nuclear risk, and other global threats. This entry asks whether a dramatic symbol can still be useful even when it is not literally precise. The section's word 'nearish' also matters: it jokes with the old phrase 'the end is nigh' while admitting that modern danger often feels close, disputed, and hard to time exactly. This is an end-zone: a period when people behave differently because the ending feels close even though it has not fully arrived.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this helps students distinguish between a symbolic warning and a real predictive instrument. At the section level, it introduces global apocalypse as something people try to imagine, measure, and communicate. At the year-theme level, it connects to prediction, endings, and thresholds because the clock dramatizes the idea of being near a point of no return. The clock is powerful because it turns a vague threshold into a public image, even while reminding students that symbolic nearness is not the same as a forecast.
entries.takeaway: An ending often begins before it happens, when people start acting as if the end is already in the room.
entries.debateRelevance: Useful in debates about political transitions, climate warnings, apocalypse culture, and who controls the story of an ending.
entries.counterargument: Some endings really are simple and final; the useful distinction is between the final moment and the approach to that moment.

items.title: The Doomsday Clock
items.note: The Doomsday Clock is best understood as a dramatic warning symbol, not a literal scientific countdown. Its power comes from making catastrophic risk feel urgent and discussable.

items.title: The end-zone
items.note: The section is interested in the period when an ending is visible but not yet complete: a leader leaving office, a clock near midnight, a school year closing, a disaster predicted but not arrived.

quizQuestions.prompt: What is the Doomsday Clock mainly meant to do?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Warn people symbolically about global danger
wrongAnswers: Predict the exact date of the end of the world
Track earthquakes in real time
Measure only nuclear explosions

quizQuestions.prompt: Why is the Doomsday Clock called symbolic rather than literal?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Because it represents danger dramatically instead of measuring exact scientific countdowns
wrongAnswers: Because it has no connection to real concerns
Because it changes every hour
Because it was built as a museum toy

quizQuestions.prompt: Why might a symbol like the Doomsday Clock still be useful?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Because a powerful image can focus public attention on threats people might otherwise ignore
wrongAnswers: Because symbols are more accurate than science
Because fear always solves political problems
Because every warning needs a deadline

quizQuestions.prompt: What is one weakness of the Doomsday Clock?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: It can seem more exact than it really is, even though it is only a symbolic judgment
wrongAnswers: It never changes over time
It deals only with weather
It is understood in exactly the same way by everyone

quizQuestions.prompt: What is the strongest idea behind the Doomsday Clock?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: A dramatic symbol can be helpful if it makes people think seriously about catastrophic risks without pretending to be a literal forecast
wrongAnswers: The best warning is always the most precise clock
Symbolic tools are useless in politics
Humanity should ignore imagined thresholds

entries.title: Why People Keep Predicting the End

links.label: List of predicted apocalyptic dates - Wikipedia
Ten notable apocalypses that didn't happen - Smithsonian

entries.studentExplanation: People have predicted the end of the world again and again: through religion, astronomy, technology, calendars, and rumors. Most of these predictions fail, but they often spread quickly because they give people a simple explanation for fear, uncertainty, or rapid change. This entry is about the emotional, social, and media patterns behind doomsday belief - not just the dates themselves. The pattern is not only fear of destruction; it is also the comfort of plot. A dated apocalypse gives history a shape, even when the prediction itself collapses.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this helps students compare different failed apocalypses and see what they have in common. At the section level, it broadens the section from official warnings to mass belief and viral fear. At the year-theme level, it connects to prediction and endings because humans are strongly drawn to stories that place them near the final threshold.
entries.takeaway: People are drawn not only to the fear of the end, but also to stories that make chaos feel meaningful and understandable.
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating why apocalyptic stories spread so easily and whether the deepest driver is fear, meaning-making, media amplification, or all three at once.
entries.counterargument: Someone could argue that repeated failed prophecies should not make people dismiss every warning, because some future dangers may still be evidence-based and real.

items.title: The Millerites and Hon-Ming Chen
items.note: This group shows how prophecy, charismatic belief, and social anxiety can turn uncertainty into a dateable ending. The pattern matters as much as the failed prediction itself.

items.title: From Halley's Comet to Y2K and the Mayan Apocalypse
items.note: These cases show that modern fear does not need religion to become apocalyptic. Astronomy, computing, and media rumor can all create the same powerful feeling that humanity is nearing a final threshold.

quizQuestions.prompt: What do many failed doomsday predictions have in common?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: They often grow during times of fear, uncertainty, or rapid change
wrongAnswers: They are always based on one religion only
They succeed when repeated often enough
They depend only on science

quizQuestions.prompt: Why do apocalyptic predictions spread so easily?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Because dramatic stories about danger are emotionally powerful and easy to share
wrongAnswers: Because people always verify them carefully first
Because the future is easy to know
Because all frightening claims are true

quizQuestions.prompt: Why can both religious movements and technological scares produce doomsday beliefs?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Because very different causes can trigger the same human need to explain danger and uncertainty
wrongAnswers: Because science and religion are exactly the same
Because technology always proves prophecy right
Because fear exists only in modern societies

quizQuestions.prompt: What does the Y2K panic show about modern apocalyptic fear?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: A technological problem can become a culture-wide symbol of collapse
wrongAnswers: Computers always cause civilizational endings
The internet invented doomsday thinking
Digital fears are less emotional than older ones

quizQuestions.prompt: What is the strongest idea behind recurring doomsday predictions?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: People are drawn not only to the fear of the end, but also to stories that make chaos feel meaningful and understandable
wrongAnswers: The end of the world is usually predicted correctly on the second try
Social media created apocalyptic thinking from nothing
Every false prophecy makes future prophecies impossible

entries.title: Renaissance Visions of the End

links.label: The Four Horsemen of the Apocalypse - Met Museum
The Triumph of Death - AF Prado Museum

entries.studentExplanation: For Renaissance artists, the apocalypse was often religious, moral, and full of judgment. These images separate the saved from the damned, but they do not all feel the same: some are full of terror, while others contain order, symbolism, or even a kind of strange calm. This entry asks students to compare how artists make doom visible and how they imagine what lies beyond the final division.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this helps students read apocalypse not just as destruction, but as visual storytelling about justice, fear, and destiny. At the section level, it adds an art-history dimension to the section's idea of endings. At the year-theme level, it connects to thresholds and endings because apocalyptic art often shows the most extreme threshold of all: final separation.
entries.takeaway: The end of the world is shown not only as catastrophe, but as a final threshold where moral order is revealed.
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether apocalyptic art mainly frightens viewers or whether it also offers moral structure, symbolism, and a larger idea of justice.
entries.counterargument: Someone could argue that these works are rooted in a specific religious tradition and may not represent a universal way of imagining endings.

items.title: Albrecht Durer - The Four Horsemen of the Apocalypse (1498)
items.note: Durer turns Revelation into compressed visual force, making the apocalypse feel immediate, crowded, and unstoppable.

items.title: Sandro Botticelli - The Mystical Nativity (1500)
items.note: Botticelli mixes fear, hope, and symbolic order, reminding students that apocalypse can also point toward spiritual meaning beyond destruction.

items.title: Michelangelo - The Last Judgment (1536-1541)
items.note: Michelangelo imagines the final division of souls as a moment of overwhelming movement, power, and moral reckoning.

items.title: Pieter Bruegel the Elder - The Triumph of Death (c. 1562)
items.note: Bruegel turns apocalypse into a landscape-scale social collapse where death moves through ordinary human life with chilling inevitability.

items.title: Extra context
items.note: This overview gives broader historical context for why apocalypse imagery remained so compelling across European visual culture.

quizQuestions.prompt: What is one common feature of many Renaissance apocalypse images?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: They divide people sharply between salvation and doom
wrongAnswers: They avoid religious ideas completely
They focus only on peaceful landscapes
They reject symbolism

quizQuestions.prompt: What makes apocalyptic art from this period more than just a picture of destruction?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: It also imagines judgment, morality, and the fate of souls
wrongAnswers: It functions only as decoration
It ignores human emotion
It rejects all stories about the future

quizQuestions.prompt: Why might two artists depict the apocalypse with very different tones?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Because the end can be imagined as terror, warning, order, justice, or even acceptance
wrongAnswers: Because Renaissance artists were not allowed to choose style
Because all religious art had one exact mood
Because tone matters only in music

quizQuestions.prompt: What does a work like The Last Judgment encourage viewers to think about?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: What kind of moral order lies behind the final separation of the saved and the damned
wrongAnswers: Whether painting is more realistic than sculpture
How to predict next year's politics
Whether all endings are beautiful

quizQuestions.prompt: What is the strongest idea behind Renaissance apocalypse art?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: The end of the world is shown not only as catastrophe, but as a final threshold where moral order is revealed
wrongAnswers: Apocalypse art is only about fear
Renaissance painters cared more about crowds than meaning
The end matters only if it is violent

entries.title: Modern Apocalypses and New Fears

links.label: The Course of Empire: Destruction - Google Arts & Culture
The Great Day of His Wrath - Tate
World War I prints - The Guardian
Four Horsemen of the Apocalypse - Nelson-Atkins
The Last of Us: Season 1 Concept Art

entries.studentExplanation: More recent apocalyptic art often shifts away from pure religious judgment and toward industrial collapse, war, disease, technology, ruined cities, and human-made disaster. These works ask what modern people fear most: mechanized destruction, total war, social breakdown, environmental collapse, digital dread, or the feeling that civilization may destroy itself. Keep the work links directly in the display area so students can move between painting, print, concept art, and music-video style storytelling without invented visuals.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this helps students compare how apocalyptic imagery evolves as history changes. At the section level, it extends the guiding section from classical and religious endings to modern fears shaped by industry, war, media, and digital anxiety. At the year-theme level, it connects to endings, prediction, and the future by showing that every era imagines doom in the image of its own deepest worries.
entries.takeaway: The end of the world increasingly appears as something tied to human systems, choices, and fears rather than only divine fate.
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether modern apocalyptic art mostly reflects public fear - or actively shapes how societies imagine collapse and the future.
entries.counterargument: Someone could argue that even modern apocalypse art still reuses older fears of judgment, destruction, and chaos rather than inventing an entirely new language of endings.

items.title: Thomas Cole - The Course of Empire: Destruction (1836)
items.note: Cole turns imperial grandeur into self-destruction, suggesting that civilizations can build the very forces that undo them.

items.title: John Martin - The Great Day of His Wrath (1851-1853)
items.note: Martin keeps the cosmic scale of apocalypse but makes catastrophe feel overwhelming, theatrical, and physically unavoidable.

items.title: Otto Dix - World War I prints (1924)
items.note: Dix makes modern war itself look apocalyptic, where industrial violence turns real history into a vision of ruin.

items.title: Viktor Schreckengost - Apocalypse '42 (1942)
items.note: This wartime work translates twentieth-century conflict into a direct visual language of anxiety, destruction, and mass fear.

items.title: Harry Louis Freund - Four Horsemen of the Apocalypse (1946)
items.note: Freund reimagines a classical apocalyptic motif after global war, showing how older symbols survive inside modern dread.

items.title: Saby Menyhei - The Last of Us: Season 1 Concept Art (2025)
items.note: The work turns post-collapse atmosphere into contemporary visual media, where fear is shaped by survival, emptiness, and damaged systems.

items.title: Kevin Sherwood & James McCawley - Where Are We Going? (2013)
items.note: This piece extends apocalyptic mood into game-linked sound and atmosphere, showing that doom can also be built through media form and tone.

quizQuestions.prompt: What is one major difference between many modern apocalyptic works and older religious ones?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Modern works often focus more on war, industry, technology, or human-made collapse
wrongAnswers: Modern works avoid fear completely
Older works were never symbolic
Modern works are always hopeful

quizQuestions.prompt: Why does industrial or digital anxiety fit modern apocalypse so well?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Because people increasingly fear systems they built but may no longer fully control
wrongAnswers: Because technology removed all uncertainty
Because only ancient societies imagined endings
Because modern art cannot show nature

quizQuestions.prompt: What can war imagery add to apocalyptic art?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: It makes the end feel historically real and human-caused instead of distant and mythical
wrongAnswers: It turns apocalypse into comedy
It removes all moral meaning
It guarantees political agreement

quizQuestions.prompt: Why might a work like The Last of Us concept art still count as apocalyptic imagery?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Because it imagines social breakdown, fear, and survival in a damaged world
wrongAnswers: Because anything recent is automatically apocalyptic
Because concept art is more factual than painting
Because apocalypse requires zombies

quizQuestions.prompt: What is the strongest idea behind modern apocalypse art?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: The end of the world increasingly appears as something tied to human systems, choices, and fears rather than only divine fate
wrongAnswers: Modern society no longer believes in endings
Technology makes apocalyptic thinking obsolete
All modern apocalypse art is just entertainment

entries.title: A Soundtrack for the End

links.label: It's the End of the World as We Know It - YouTube

entries.studentExplanation: Apocalypse is not only something artists paint; it is also something composers and musicians can make us hear. These works turn endings into sound in very different ways: Messiaen uses stillness, space, and spiritual intensity; Black Sabbath turns doom into heavy, crushing power; and R.E.M. makes catastrophe feel frantic, crowded, and strangely catchy. This entry helps students notice that mood, instrumentation, tempo, repetition, and silence can all shape what an ending feels like.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this helps students understand how music creates apocalyptic atmosphere. At the section level, it broadens the guiding section beyond visual art and prophecy into sound and emotional tone. At the year-theme level, it connects to endings and the future by showing that fear of collapse can be expressed not just through images and stories, but through rhythm, texture, and musical form.
entries.takeaway: The end of the world can be heard through very different musical strategies, each shaping a different emotional version of catastrophe.
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether music communicates crisis more effectively through mood and texture than essays or images can do alone.
entries.counterargument: Someone could argue that apocalyptic music is too subjective without historical context, because listeners may hear very different meanings in the same sounds.

items.title: Olivier Messiaen - Abyss of the Birds (1940)
items.note: Messiaen uses stillness, suspended time, and spiritual intensity to make catastrophe feel quiet, vast, and almost outside ordinary history.

items.title: Black Sabbath - Electric Funeral (1970)
items.note: Heavy riffs, slow drive, and dark tone make apocalypse sound industrial, crushing, and physically oppressive.

items.title: R.E.M. - It's the End of the World as We Know It (1987)
items.note: Speed, overload, and catchiness turn collapse into breathless cultural anxiety rather than solemn doom.

quizQuestions.prompt: What can music add to apocalyptic storytelling?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: A strong emotional atmosphere created through sound, rhythm, silence, and texture
wrongAnswers: A precise scientific forecast
A visual map of destruction
A guarantee that the message will be optimistic

quizQuestions.prompt: Why might very different kinds of music all fit an apocalyptic theme?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Because endings can feel spiritual, terrifying, chaotic, heavy, or strangely calm
wrongAnswers: Because all apocalypse music sounds the same
Because only sad music can discuss disaster
Because lyrics matter but sound never does

quizQuestions.prompt: What makes a piece like Abyss of the Birds especially unsettling?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Its slowness, space, and suspended feeling can make time itself feel fragile
wrongAnswers: It uses only loud percussion
It sounds like dance music
It avoids mood completely

quizQuestions.prompt: Why can a song like It's the End of the World as We Know It sound energetic and anxious at the same time?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Because speed and overload can make collapse feel breathless rather than solemn
wrongAnswers: Because fast songs cannot express fear
Because catchy music removes serious meaning
Because apocalypse works only in classical music

quizQuestions.prompt: What is the strongest idea behind apocalyptic music?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: The end of the world can be heard through very different musical strategies, each shaping a different emotional version of catastrophe
wrongAnswers: Only lyrics determine apocalyptic meaning
Music is less powerful than visual art for endings
Apocalyptic music must always be slow and dark

entries.title: The Point of No Return

links.label: Point of no return debate - The Ecologist

entries.studentExplanation: Researchers studying ecosystems have found that collapse is often preceded by instability: instead of staying steady, systems start swinging more wildly. Climate change may be producing similar warning signs, from more extreme weather to rougher flights and deeper environmental stress. This entry asks what people should do when a danger no longer feels far away. The hardest question is not only whether a point of no return exists, but how humans should respond if they think they are approaching it.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this helps students connect environmental science to the idea of collapse. At the section level, it turns apocalyptic thinking away from fantasy and toward measurable real-world danger. At the year-theme level, it connects to endings, prediction, and the future by asking what responsibility looks like when a system may already be close to irreversible change.
entries.takeaway: Even when full prevention seems uncertain, warning signs of instability matter because they shape how urgently people should act.
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether societies should keep acting aggressively even when some damage may already be irreversible.
entries.counterargument: Someone could argue that calling a situation a point of no return too early can become an excuse for surrender when meaningful reduction of harm is still possible.

items.title: Predicting ecosystem collapse
items.note: Researchers study volatility and instability because systems often become more erratic before they break down.

items.title: Climate volatility research
items.note: Climate change can look chaotic before it looks final, with stronger swings and more unstable patterns across systems.

items.title: Turbulence and climate connection
items.note: Even everyday experiences like flying can become reminders that environmental instability is already affecting human systems.

items.title: Point of no return debate
items.note: The moral question is not only whether a threshold exists, but what people owe one another if they believe they are nearing it.

quizQuestions.prompt: What is one warning sign that an ecosystem may be close to collapse?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Its behavior can become more volatile and unstable
wrongAnswers: It always becomes perfectly calm first
It stops changing completely
It becomes easier to control

quizQuestions.prompt: Why does the phrase point of no return sound so powerful in climate discussions?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Because it suggests there may be a threshold after which damage becomes much harder or impossible to undo
wrongAnswers: Because it proves the future is already fixed
Because science has ended
Because it applies only to lakes

quizQuestions.prompt: Why might climate change be described as chaotic before catastrophic?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Because systems often become more unstable and extreme before they fully break down
wrongAnswers: Because catastrophe always arrives without warning
Because chaos and climate are unrelated
Because only politics creates instability

quizQuestions.prompt: What ethical question appears if people believe the climate fight may already be partly lost?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Whether reducing damage still matters even if total prevention may no longer be possible
wrongAnswers: Whether science should stop measuring change
Whether weather should be left alone
Whether only rich countries should respond

quizQuestions.prompt: What is the strongest idea behind this climate-collapse entry?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Even when full prevention seems uncertain, warning signs of instability matter because they shape how urgently people should act
wrongAnswers: A point of no return means action becomes meaningless
Environmental collapse is only symbolic
Climate anxiety matters more than evidence

entries.title: Profiting from Disaster
entries.studentExplanation: Some people do not only fear climate breakdown - they look for ways to profit from it. That raises a difficult question: when does preparing for crisis become benefiting from crisis? This entry is not just about investment. It is about ethics, incentives, and what happens when money begins to reward the expectation of harm. A company or government does not need to say 'we want disaster' to create a system that quietly benefits when disaster grows worse.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this helps students think about the moral problem of profit during crisis. At the section level, it gives the guiding section a sharp contemporary ending by showing how apocalypse can become an economic opportunity. At the year-theme level, it connects to endings and the future by asking whether some systems may become invested in decline instead of prevention.
entries.takeaway: If disaster becomes profitable, society risks creating incentives that normalize or even deepen the very dangers it should resist.
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating where the line sits between responsible preparation for crisis and a system that starts rewarding the expectation of harm.
entries.counterargument: Someone could argue that some investment during crisis can still be ethical if it reduces damage, improves resilience, or funds adaptation rather than exploiting suffering.

items.title: When crisis becomes profitable
items.note: This entry asks whether preparing for disaster can slide into quietly benefiting from disaster. The ethical pressure comes from incentives: once harm becomes profitable, prevention may no longer be everyone's goal.

quizQuestions.prompt: What is the core ethical problem in profiting from climate failure?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Someone may gain financially from worsening conditions that harm others
wrongAnswers: Profit is always unethical
Climate change has no economic side
All investors cause disasters directly

quizQuestions.prompt: Why is there a difference between preparing for disaster and profiting from it?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Preparation can reduce harm, while profit-seeking may create incentives to accept or exploit harm
wrongAnswers: There is no difference at all
Preparation matters only for governments
Profit automatically solves crisis

quizQuestions.prompt: What makes climate-profit opportunities politically troubling?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: They may reward people for adapting to collapse instead of preventing it
wrongAnswers: They always lower temperatures
They remove all inequality
They make long-term planning unnecessary

quizQuestions.prompt: Why might a government or company be tempted to benefit from crisis rather than stop it?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Because systems can be shaped by incentives that reward short-term gain over long-term safety
wrongAnswers: Because disasters are easier to predict than profits
Because ethics plays no role in policy
Because prevention is always impossible

quizQuestions.prompt: What is the strongest idea behind this final entry?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: If disaster becomes profitable, society risks creating incentives that normalize or even deepen the very dangers it should resist
wrongAnswers: All economic activity during crisis is exploitation
Markets cannot respond ethically to danger
Climate change matters only when investors notice it
